here we have a very simple flask

and in that route recalling a function

always going to return none if we were
to run this in the normal way we would
see that it would tell us that we have
an assertion error inside of our code
there we go not too great The thing that
is often scary is when you run this in
an optimized mode so python has an
optimized mode using the dash o operator
and if we were to run that and reload
the page we would see that that
statement is completely ignored
the reason for this is because when you
run python in an optimized mode using
the dash o argument what happens is all
the asserts in your application are
removed which can lead to some pretty
catastrophic results now python is often
not run using optimized mode because
most often you don't need it but in case
you wanted to save a little bit of
memory and you have to distribute python
to a lot of different machines this can
be quite helpful alright folks it's time
for number two and that's joining paths
this might come as a surprise but
joining paths in Python might not be as
intuitive as you think it is so here in
this example what we're going to do is
import OS because we're going to use the
os.path module and we're going to
declare two paths that we will want to
join in the future
all right so we're going to hit
os.path.join and something interesting
is going to happen which is that the
pads are not going to be joined
which isn't what we wanted and that's
not great
so what we need to do is actually get
rid of the forward slash at the
beginning of the path and if we had a
Windows machine this would be backward
slashes and now if we were to join those
two together we would see that those two
can be joined and are joined now you
might say well nafio I don't really use
os.path I use pathlib and I use the path
object well if you were to do the same
thing with path objects you would find
yourself in the same sort of conundrum
so here if we were to join those two
paths together we would just get the
same as what we did when we first
attempted to join paths using
os.path.join and if we were to use the
method
on the path object as well we would have
the same results
and that's not exactly what we wanted
now if we were to get rid of the forward
slash at the beginning we would get what
we wanted in the first place
so when dealing with paths make sure
that the forward slash
at the front he's gotten rid of this
particular Quirk has caused trouble for
eight one a web-based terminal emulator
and it was actually a cve back in 2020.
so be careful about how you deal with
your paths okay so this one is called
the zip slip now the zip slip is
basically where you sneak in a file
within a zip file that is actually
referring to a directory that is out of
your current working directory so to
demonstrate that I'm going to show you
this little file so here we have a main
function and in this main function we
are going to use zip file.zip file to
extract a zip file
so here
we are using for entry in zf.name list
and we're going to say hey if the entry
ends with DOT txt we want to read it and
then we want to write it
okay seems harmless enough
but I'm going to show you something that
is kind of scary
so I'm going to run this in debug mode
and you're going to see one of these
things is referring to
a place that is outside of our current
working directory so this if we were to
create it in the way that we are now
this would be created outside of our
working directory and this is one of the
ways in which malicious attacks can
happen so in order to mitigate this what
you want to do is use extract which is
what I'm going to do so here I'm just
going to replace all of this with
zf.extract so zip file.extract and I'm
going to tell which place to put the
file in and once we've run that program
we can see that inside a place to
extract there is only good.txt and
anything that has to do or refers to
anything outside of our working
directory is ignored and this is because
extract and extract all our methods that
try to prevent the zip slip attack from
happening the next one is a problem in a
similar vein to the last so this is
about the temp file module that we have
in Python and what interesting about
this module is that it can create a
named temporary file so over here you
can see that I have something called a
prefix and this is something that is
prepended to the file name and this is
the keyword argument that is susceptible
to a path traversal attack you can put
in a path that tries to access a
directory right above the one that
you're creating the temp file in so for
example if we were to run this request
you would see that we have created this
text file over here that's right above
this particular directory
now in most places you should have
permissions in place to prevent access
to anything sensitive however this does
act as a vector for attack when people
want to exploit your system this
particular kind of issue is called the
path traversal attack or the directory
traversal attack this is something that
you want to avoid so make sure that you
always sanitize any input that's coming
in and be careful what you plug into the
prefix variable of your temp files okay
so for the last but not least member of
the Lesser known python security
pitfalls is IP address normalization now
if you take a look at here we have the
IP address library and what we're going
to do is we're going to send a request
to an IP that we get from the user
now the user is a little malicious over
here you can see that instead of writing
an IP address normally they have chosen
to write the IP in an abnormal kind of
way but you're going to see something
interesting happen when this gets
normalized so if we were to run this
with python 3.7 you are going to see
that we actually did try to send a
request to a local address let's add a
little more clarity and I'm going to do
that by printing out the normalized
version of the ip4 address that we are
getting from the ip4 method and then
running it again in Python 3.7
we see that the IP is normalized to
127.0.0.1 and that means that the server
is making an internal request however
this line fails to catch that because
there are two zeros here instead of one
and what eventually happens
is that this gets normalized by the IP
address library and eventually the
request gets sent
so what you want to do is use the latest
version of python whenever you can but
if you're using a version of python
that's less than 3.8 you can be
susceptible to this kind of attack where
user input is sending you a malicious IP
address that will eventually be used to
attack one of your own internal servers
or services
and with that thank you so much for
watching I hope to see you again in the
next video
